coredata.dc:title: Modeling of precipitation hardening during the aging and overaging of 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 300 steel
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Pardal, J.M.
Tavares, S.S.M.
Terra, V.F.
Da Silva, M.R.
Dos Santos, D.R.
coredata.dc:description:
The precipitation hardening behavior of an 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 300 solution treated and aged in the 440–650°C range was investigated. The aging at 440, 480, 510 and 560°C was modeled by an equation ΔH =(Kt) n . The activation energy for precipitation was determined (132kJ/mol) and compared to published values for 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 250 and 350 steels. The model for overaging based on the Orowan mechanism was tested in samples treated at 560, 600 and 650°C but was found to be valid only for 560°C. The softening of samples aged at 600 and 650°C is rather due to austenite precipitation than to Ni3(Mo, Ti) and Fe2Mo coarsening. Equations H = H 1 t
p were fitted for the overaging at 560, 600 and 650°C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Maraging steels
Overaging
Precipitation hardening

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hardness vs. aging time at 440, 480, 510 and 560°C.
ln(ΔH) vs. ln(t) for the samples aged at 440, 480, 510 and 560°C.
X-ray diffractogram of the sample aged at 560°C for 45min.
ln(ΔH) vs. ln(t) for the samples aged at 440°C using two fitting lines.
Plot of ln(t) vs. 1/T for determination of the activation energy for precipitation.
Hardness vs. aging time at 560, 600 and 650°C.
Plots of (ΔH)−3 vs. aging time at 560 and 600°C for modeling of the overaging kinetics.
X-ray diffractogram of the sample aged at 510°C for 24h.
Comparison between the X-ray diffractograms of the samples aged at 510°C for 24h, 560°C for 1h, 600°C for 1h and 650°C for 15min.
Fittings of the hardness vs. aging time curves at 560, 600 and 650°C in the overaged portion.
Chemical composition of the maraging steel studied
n and K values at 440°C, 480°C, 510°C and 560°C
M and ΔH 0 values at 560 and 600°C

article.ce:title: Modeling of precipitation hardening during the aging and overaging of 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 300 steel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Engenharia Mecânica – PGMEC – UFF – Rua Passo da Pátria 156, CEP 24210-240, Niterói RJ, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UNIFEI/Instituto de Ciências, Itajubá MG, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro Tecnológico da Aeronáutica – CTA, São José dos Campos SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The precipitation hardening behavior of an 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 300 solution treated and aged in the 440–650°C range was investigated. The aging at 440, 480, 510 and 560°C was modeled by an equation ΔH =(Kt) n . The activation energy for precipitation was determined (132kJ/mol) and compared to published values for 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 250 and 350 steels. The model for overaging based on the Orowan mechanism was tested in samples treated at 560, 600 and 650°C but was found to be valid only for 560°C. The softening of samples aged at 600 and 650°C is rather due to austenite precipitation than to Ni3(Mo, Ti) and Fe2Mo coarsening. Equations H = H 1 t
p were fitted for the overaging at 560, 600 and 650°C.

ce:section.ce:para: Maraging steels are age hardenable alloys for special purposes, where the combination of high strength and good toughness is required. The microstructure of the as quenched 18% maraging steel consists of low carbon and high nickel and cobalt martensite. The strengthening in low temperature aging (<450°C) is produced by ordered and coherent phases such as μ, S and X phases [1–3]
The aging above 450°C produces an intense hardening due to the precipitation of Ni3(Mo, Ti) and Fe2Mo phases [1–3].

Aging between 500°C and the A s temperature produces austenite precipitation by a diffusion controlled reaction [4–5]. According to Li and Yin [6] the austenite formation occurs at the same moment and as consequence of the partial dissolution of Ni3(Ti, Mo) and precipitation of Fe2Mo. Depending on the martensite start temperature (Ms), the austenite formed by this way may be retained, partially or totally transformed into martensite during the cooling to room temperature. Ms decreases with the nickel content of the austenite, which is found to decrease with the aging temperature [7–8]. As a consequence, Ms increases with the aging temperature above 500°C. The amount of austenite observed at room temperature increases with the aging temperature till a peak value and then decreases as the temperature Ms decreases. The maximum amount of reverted austenite observed at room temperature in 18Ni Co-containing maraging steels is obtained by aging treatments between 620 and 670°C [9].

Wilson [10] presented and applied models for aging and overaging of precipitation hardenable alloys. Sha [11] applied these models for a maraging C-300 steel aged at 510°C. The equation used for aging is based on the Jonhson–Mehl and Avrami model [12–13]. The equation proposed for overaging is based on the Orowan mechanism. In the present work, these equations were tested for an 18Ni–Co–Mo–Ti maraging 300 steel aged in the 440–650°C range.

A 2.5mm thick sheet of maraging 300 steel (composition shown in Table 1 ) was solution treated at 900°C for 40min. After this the samples were aged at 440, 480, 510, 560 and 650°C for different times from 15min to 24h. All heat treatments were conducted in vacuum-sealed quartz tubes.

Vickers hardness tests and X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) were carried out in all conditions. All the X-ray measurements were carried out using a diffractometer PHILIPS®, model X’Pert Pro, in step scan mode with step size of 0.02° and time per step of 3s. Cu Kα (1.54056Å) radiation was used with 40kV and 40mA. In order to keep the beam completely on the sample for low incident angles, a divergence slit of 1° was used.

Fig. 1 shows the precipitation hardening curves of the maraging 300 steel aged at 440, 480, 510 and 560°C. The aging curves at 440 and 480°C do not present overaging till 24h. The maximum hardness is obtained by the aging at 480°C for 10 and 24h. The samples aged at 510°C achieve a maximum hardness of 629HV at 4h of aging, and undergo a slight overaging after this.

The model for kinetics of early stages of aging of precipitation hardenable alloys is given by an expression derived from the Avrami [13] equation [10]:
(1)
Δ
H =
( K t )
n
where K is the temperature dependent rate constant; n the time exponent, slightly temperature dependent; ΔH the increase of hardening (HV); t the aging time (h)

Table 2 shows the n and K values obtained by plotting ln(ΔH) versus ln(t) (Fig. 2 ). The n values obtained at 440, 480 and 510°C are close, but the one obtained at 560°C is smaller. This is attributed to the softening effect caused by austenite precipitation at this temperature, even before the overaging. Fig. 3 shows the X-ray diffractogram of the sample aged at 560°C by 45min with austenite peaks.

The n and K values obtained at 510°C in this work (Table 2) are somewhat different from the ones obtained by Sha [11] in a maraging steel of the same type. This can be attributed to differences in the time range used to fit the curves. In this work, we took points between 15min and 2h, while Sha [11] analyzed the hardening between 160s and 25min.

The activation energy for precipitation may be obtained by plotting the logarithm of the time taken to achieve the peak hardness against 1/T. The results may be fitted by an Arhenius type equation:
(2)
ln ⁡ ( t ) =
Q
RT
+ constant
where Q is activation energy for the precipitation (kJ/mol); t the time taken to attain peak hardness at each temperature (h); R the universal gas constant (0.008314kJ/molK); T the aging temperature (K).

Wilson [10] and Sha [11] have also proposed a model for overaging based on the Orowan mechanism:
(3)
1
Δ
H
3
= M ( t −
t 0
) +
1
Δ
H 0
3
where ΔH is difference between the hardening of the overaged sample and the solution treated one (HV); M the temperature dependent rate constant; t 0 the time at peak hardness (h); ΔH 0 the increase in hardness at commencement of coarsening time t 0 (HV).

Fig. 6 shows the precipitation hardening curves of the maraging 300 steel aged at 560, 600 and 650°C and Fig. 7 shows the fittings (ΔH)−3 versus aging time at 560 and 600°C used to obtain the parameters M and ΔH 0 presented in Table 4 . The correlation coefficients (R) of the linear fittings are also shown. The comparison between the ΔH 0 obtained by the fitting with the experimental value also gives an idea of the validity of the model. The work of Sha [11] shows the application of Eq. (3) on the overaging of a Cr-containing maraging steel at 520°C. At this temperature, he obtained a good correlation coefficient (R 2 =0.98) with the modeled ΔH 0 (140HV) very close to the experimental value (141HV). In the present work, the model applied at 560°C presents satisfactory results, with R =0.99 (R 2 =0.98) and ΔH 0 (253HV) close to the experimental value (257HV). The modeling at 600 and 650°C does not give good results. At 600°C, the correlation coefficient is very low (R =0.91) and the determined ΔH 0 value (184 HV) is very different from the experimental value (207HV). The fitting at 650°C (not shown) gives an R =0.97 and a negative ΔH 0, which has no physical meaning.

The overaging of the maraging steel aged in temperatures higher than 500°C is enhanced by the austenite formation. Fig. 8 shows the X-ray diffractogram of the sample aged at 510°C for 24h with austenite peaks. However, despite this, satisfactory fittings are obtained at 520 [11] and 560°C (this work). Fig. 9 shows that the amounts of austenite formed at 600 and 650°C are much higher than at 560 and 510°C, which seems to be the reason, why Eq. (3) does not describe well the overaging at 600 and 650°C.

On the other hand, the overaging of the maraging 300 steel at 560, 600 and 650°C can be mathematically described by an equation of the type (Fig. 10 ):
H ( t ) =
H 1
t p
where H is hardness value (HV); t the aging time (h); H 1 the hardness value at t =1h; p the negative exponent of time.

Fig. 10 shows the curves fitted for 560, 600 and 650°C. Table 5 shows the parameters of the equations (H 1 and p) and the correlation coefficients obtained. The H 1 obtained by the model and the experimental values (also shown in Table 5) are very close, which validates the model.

The age hardening of the maraging 300 steel between 440 and 560°C can be modeled by the equation ΔH =(kt) n with good correlation coefficients (R). The n values found at 440, 480 and 510°C were 0.20, 0.20 and 0.21, respectively. The n value falls to 0.16 at the aging temperature of 560°C, which is attributed to the influence of austenite formation during the aging at this temperature. The aging at 440°C could also be modeled by two curves, representing different stages of precipitation.
An activation energy for precipitation hardening of 132±10kJ/mol was calculated by the Arhenius equation. This value is between the ones obtained in maraging 250 [15] and maraging 350 [14] steels.

The model proposed by Wilson [10] for overaging was tested for the samples aged at 560, 600 and 650°C, but was only valid at 560°C. The overaging at 650, 600°C is rather due to the softening effect of the intense austenite formation, and for this reason, is not described by the equation proposed by Wilson [10]. On the other hand, the overaging at 560, 600 and 650°C was well described by an equation H = H 1·t
p , where H 1 is the hardness value for t =1h and p is a negative expoent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Prof. Hamilton F.G. De Abreu (UFC/Brazil) for the X-ray diffraction facilities and the Brazilian research agencies (CAPES, FAPERJ and CNPq) for financial support.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Magnée, J.M. Drapier, J. Dumont, D. Coutsouradis, L. Habraken, Cobalt-containing high-strength steels, Centre d’Information du Cobalt, Brussels, 1974.
